column3: Complete Series A in Q1 to fund growth
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Secure $1-2M Bridge with MES#CAP and Secure Series A Tranche Funding Milestones ($1M in 45 days; $3M in 90 days) with Keiretsu.
Done looks like:
$1M (Bridge 1 tranche) before Feb 28th. Optionally 2 more tranches of $500K before Series A if needed.
$1M (Series A 1st payment) of the tranche is received within 45 days.
$3M (Series A 2nd tranche) is fully completed and received within 90 days.

column3: Stabilize and refactor core UX flows for product performance
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Optimize Quote Path + Money-Flow Journeys to Improve Order Completion and optimize the onboarding process.
Done looks like:
Quote path (first-order processing) and all key in-app user journeys—with priority on money flows—are enhanced and released.
≥ 50% of users successfully complete onboarding.
≥ 50% of first-time orders result in a fully completed transaction.
≥ 90% of all orders complete without customer service intervention.
The remaining ≤ 10% of orders are successfully resolved by customer service within the agreed SLA.

column3: Release m2m to support Kapital deployment in the baseline
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Launch Me2Me with Kapital in Production (Baseline Push + Pull) and hit conversion + reliability targets.
Done looks like:
Me2Me with Kapital is live in production and included in the baseline for both Push and Pull flows.
The end-to-end user experience achieves ≥ 25% conversion for new users.
The system processes ≥ 90% of all transactions successfully (as defined by your success criteria) over an agreed measurement window (e.g., trailing 2 weeks post-launch).

column3: Maximize Payment Methods Acceptance to decrease friction
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Validate All Known Payment Use Cases in Production to Improve Error Handling and User Success (QA + R&D + Risk + Product)
Done looks like:
Analysis of current payment methods issues ongoing
CRB Card Information Not Complete / Not Usefull Issues
CRB Issues in PROD with Push Transactions (declined countries)
Arcus Account Ownership Verification issues
A complete use-case coverage map is created and agreed with R&D, Risk, and Product (owned test plan with scope, scenarios, and acceptance criteria).
QA executes production testing for all known use cases, including negative/error paths and recovery flows.
We produce a prioritized, actionable output package:
List of enhancements (UX + flow improvements)
List of bug fixes (with severity, reproduction steps, and owners)
Catalog of exceptions that require Customer Service handling, with clear guidance/runbooks for each.
Success criteria are defined and reported (e.g., pass rate by use case, top error categories, and recommended fixes).

Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Standardize and Automate Production Releases + Client Configuration for Reliable, Repeatable Deployments (Zero Manual Error)
Done looks like:
Production releases and client configurations are executed through a standard, repeatable deployment process owned by Cloud Operations with minimal dependency on R&D.
Deployment and configuration are driven by structured forms/systems (validated inputs, approvals, audit trail) with no free-text / ad-hoc steps.
Cloud Operations has a complete deployment toolkit:
automation scripts / pipelines for releases + config changes
checklists and runbooks (pre-checks, deploy, rollback, post-checks)
clear ownership and handoffs (RACI)
Confidence criteria are met:
0 critical production incidents attributable to deployment/configuration errors over the agreed measurement window
100% of deployments follow the standardized process (tracked)
Rollback procedure tested and documented for Tier 1 releases.

Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Redesign Cards API + Certification Model to Enforce Compliance While Reducing Dev Burden (Stable Docs, Sandbox, Samples, Reference Implementation)
Done looks like:
The Cards API is refactored so compliance enforcement is primarily achieved through a certification program (not constant API-level business-logic changes), reducing ongoing partner/client rework.
The API surface is simplified and stabilized (versioning + change policy defined) to minimize updates when business rules evolve.
A complete developer enablement package is delivered:
well-structured, modern online documentation (guides + reference + error catalog)
a fully functional sandbox that supports end-to-end testing of core card flows GALILEO CV - THIS IS IHOW IT WORKS TODAY
ready-to-run samples (agreed languages)
a complete reference implementation (e.g., “Cloud Cards”) that Leap can deploy internally and/or provide for clients to use.
Certification is live with clear artifacts:
certification criteria + test suite - DONE VANESA
process/workflow (enroll → test → pass/fail → recert when needed)
owners and SLAs for support and approvals.

column3: Enhance the Partner API by removing sequencing requirements
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Refactor Partner API to Simplify Integration (Maintain Validations) + Launch Certification and Modern Developer Experience
Done looks like:
A refactored simpler Partner API is released that maintains required validations while removing rigid “recipe” sequencing (developers can integrate with fewer mandatory steps).
A formal certification process is defined and operational (criteria, test suite, environments, and pass/fail workflow).
Modern, online documentation is live and complete (reference + guides), including:
clear quickstart and integration paths
ready-to-use sample apps and code snippets
multi-language support options (SDKs or examples for agreed languages)
troubleshooting/error catalog and best practices
Developers have ready-to-run samples that successfully complete core flows end-to-end in the sandbox (and certify against the test suite).

column3: Create Golive to Scale Playbook to boost partner performance
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Build and Launch a Go-Live-to-Scale Methodology (Profile Use Cases → 500 TX/day within 12 months)
Done looks like:
A clear, repeatable method exists to profile partner use cases that align with revenue targets and define the path from 1 → 100 TX/day.
The Go-Live to Scale process is formally defined to start before RCGA and be considered complete only when the account reaches 500 TX/day, with a maximum timeline of 12 months from go-live (or RCGA, per your internal definition).
By end of Q1, the methodology is operational with:
documented stages, gates, and entry/exit criteria (incl. 1, 100, 500 TX/day)
required artifacts (templates, checklists, playbooks, dashboards)
clear owners/RACI and resourcing aligned across teams
pilot readiness: at least 1 partner/account selected to run through the process end-to-end.

column3: Optimize Order to Cash to reduce delivery time and increase reliability
column5:
Rock Statement (EOS)
Rock: Optimize Order-to-Cash to Reduce Delivery Time and Increase Reliability (R&D + Cloud Ops + Sales)
Done looks like:
A cross-functional Order-to-Cash process is defined, documented, and operating reliably across R&D, Cloud Operations, and Sales.
Tier 1 apps are ready for client testing within 2 weeks.
Zero configuration issues in Tier 1 app delivery (as defined by your defect criteria).
All Leap-side steps required to be “ready” are completed within 4 weeks.
The first live transaction occurs within 5 weeks after KOM.
App skin application and configuration are automated (repeatable, push-button, and version-controlled).
